era_context: Mid-to-late 17th century: European colonial expansion accelerating; the Qing Dynasty consolidating power in China following the Kangxi Emperor's accession in 1661. The Three Feudatories Rebellion (1673–1681) ongoing in southern China. Louis XIV's France at the height of its power; the Ottoman Empire in gradual decline. Religious tensions in Europe and the colonies remain high.

events.title: King Philip's War begins in New England
events.description: Metacom (King Philip), leader of the Wampanoag Confederacy, launched a coordinated uprising against English colonial settlements in New England. The war was triggered by decades of English encroachment on Native American lands, broken treaties, and the execution of three Wampanoag men in 1675. The conflict became the deadliest war per capita in North American history, with approximately 600 English colonists and 3,000 Native Americans killed. The war lasted until 1676 and effectively ended indigenous political sovereignty in southern New England.

sources.name: Colonial military records and correspondence from Massachusetts Bay Colony and Connecticut
William Hubbard, A Narrative of the Troubles with the Indians in New-England (1677)

events.certainty_note: The war's outbreak in 1675 is well-documented in colonial records. The execution of the three Wampanoag men is dated to June 1675.

events.title: Three Feudatories Rebellion continues in Qing China
events.description: The Three Feudatories Rebellion, which began in 1673, continued to challenge Qing central authority in southern China. The rebellion involved three semi-autonomous military governors (Wu Sangui, Shang Kexi, and Geng Jingmao) who had been granted control of southern territories during the conquest of Ming China. The Kangxi Emperor's decision to revoke their titles sparked the uprising. By 1675, the rebellion was in full swing, with significant military campaigns ongoing. The conflict would not be fully suppressed until 1681, but 1675 marked the peak of the rebellion's military threat.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and military archives
Historiographic consensus from modern Qing scholarship

events.certainty_note: The rebellion's progression is well-documented in Qing records. Dates are secure.

events.title: Battle of Fehrbellin (Great Elector defeats Sweden)
events.description: Frederick William, the Great Elector of Brandenburg-Prussia, defeated a Swedish army at Fehrbellin in June 1675. This victory marked a turning point in the Scanian War (1675–1679) and demonstrated Brandenburg-Prussia's emerging military strength. The victory enhanced Frederick William's prestige and contributed to the rise of Prussia as a major European power. The battle also signaled the decline of Swedish military dominance in Northern Europe.

sources.name: Contemporary military records from Brandenburg-Prussia and Sweden
Historiographic consensus on the Scanian War

events.certainty_note: The battle date (June 1675) and outcome are well-documented in military records.

events.title: Scanian War begins between Sweden and Brandenburg-Prussia
events.description: The Scanian War (1675–1679) began as a conflict between Sweden and Brandenburg-Prussia, with Denmark and other powers becoming involved. The war was triggered by disputes over territorial control in the Baltic region and Scandinavia. Sweden, under Charles XI, sought to maintain its dominance, while Brandenburg-Prussia and Denmark sought to challenge Swedish hegemony. The war resulted in significant territorial changes and contributed to the shift in the balance of power in Northern Europe away from Sweden.

sources.name: Diplomatic and military records from Sweden, Brandenburg-Prussia, and Denmark

events.certainty_note: The war's outbreak in 1675 is well-documented in diplomatic records.

events.title: Leibniz develops calculus (independent of Newton)
events.description: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz developed the foundations of calculus independently of Isaac Newton, though Newton's work predated Leibniz's by several years. Leibniz's notation and approach (particularly the integral and differential notation) proved more useful for subsequent mathematical development. The priority dispute between Newton and Leibniz became one of the most contentious in the history of science, with significant consequences for the development of mathematics in Britain versus continental Europe.

sources.name: Historiographic consensus on the history of calculus

events.certainty_note: Leibniz's development of calculus occurred over several years (1675–1676). The date 1675 is approximate for the beginning of this work.

disconfirming_evidence: King Philip's War is sometimes romanticized as a unified Native American resistance movement, but modern scholarship emphasizes the diversity of Native American responses—some groups allied with the English, others remained neutral, and the 'confederacy' was fragile. The war's outcome was not inevitable; English victory depended on Native American divisions and English military adaptation. The Three Feudatories Rebellion is sometimes portrayed as a simple power grab by regional warlords, but modern scholarship recognizes the complex legitimacy claims of the feudatories and the genuine administrative challenges the Kangxi Emperor faced. Leibniz's calculus development is sometimes dated to 1676 rather than 1675, reflecting uncertainty in the historical record about when his key insights occurred.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1675 is rich in European and colonial North American sources. Chinese sources on the Three Feudatories Rebellion are abundant but less frequently synthesized in English-language historiography. The scientific work of Leibniz is well-documented in his manuscripts and correspondence, but the exact dating of key insights remains subject to scholarly debate. African, South Asian, and Southeast Asian events are underrepresented in available sources.

graph_edges.note: King Philip's War continued into 1676, concluding with Metacom's death
Battle of Fehrbellin was part of the broader Scanian War
Leibniz's calculus development led to the Newton-Leibniz priority dispute
